heroOverview: Eumycetoma is a chronic, progressive subcutaneous fungal infection caused by the traumatic implantation of filamentous fungi into the skin, most commonly Madurella mycetomatis, producing a characteristic clinical triad of a painless swelling, draining sinuses, and colored fungal grain discharge. It is the fungal form of mycetoma — a WHO-designated neglected tropical disease — and is responsible for approximately 70% of all mycetoma cases in endemic regions such as Sudan, where population-level infection rates in some areas reach 0.3–1 per 1,000. Unlike the closely related actinomycetoma (caused by filamentous bacteria), eumycetoma responds poorly to antibiotics and requires prolonged antifungal therapy with itraconazole or voriconazole, often combined with surgery; cure rates without surgery rarely exceed 30–35%, and amputation rates remain high in late-presenting cases.

keyStats.prevalence: Exact global burden unknown; Sudan reports the highest burden with over 6,500 documented cases; estimated 8,000–10,000 cases globally seeking care annually (WHO, 2023)
keyStats.demographics: Most common in young adult men aged 20–40 engaged in agricultural or outdoor labor; male-to-female ratio approximately 3:1 in endemic series
keyStats.avgAge: Peak incidence in adults aged 20–40 years; rare in children
keyStats.globalCases: Endemic in the mycetoma belt: Sudan, Somalia, India, Mexico, Senegal, Mauritania, and Yemen; sporadic cases reported globally in travelers and immigrants

definition: Eumycetoma (ICD-10: B47.0) is a chronic, granulomatous subcutaneous infection caused by the traumatic introduction of exogenous filamentous fungi into the skin and soft tissues, typically of the foot, lower leg, or hand. It belongs to the broader category of mycetoma — along with actinomycetoma (caused by filamentous bacteria of the order Actinomycetales) — characterized by the triad of a subcutaneous mass, draining sinuses, and grain discharge. In eumycetoma, the grains are composed of compact aggregates of fungal hyphae embedded in a cement-like matrix. The most common eumycetoma agent worldwide is Madurella mycetomatis, followed by Trematosphaeria grisea (formerly Madurella grisea), Falciformispora senegalensis, Leptosphaeria senegalensis, and Scedosporium boydii (Pseudallescheria boydii). The color of the grains is diagnostically important: M. mycetomatis produces black grains, Scedosporium boydii white-to-yellow grains. The infection progresses slowly from the inoculation site through subcutaneous tissue, fascia, muscle, and bone over months to years; the feet and lower extremities are involved in approximately 70% of cases, reflecting the barefoot agricultural exposure pattern. Eumycetoma is classified as a neglected tropical disease by the WHO and primarily affects rural agricultural communities in the tropical and subtropical mycetoma belt between 15°S and 30°N latitude. Unlike actinomycetoma, eumycetoma does not respond to standard antibacterial therapy and requires prolonged antifungal treatment, often combined with surgical debulking.

typesClassification.description: Caused most often by Madurella mycetomatis; produces dark brown to black grains in sinus discharge; the most common form globally and in sub-Saharan Africa, associated with particularly poor antifungal response and higher amputation rates.

Caused by organisms including Scedosporium boydii (Pseudallescheria boydii), Acremonium species, and Fusarium species; produces white, yellow, or pale grains; more common in the Americas and some parts of South Asia; may respond better to some antifungal agents.

Rare hematogenous spread beyond the primary site, reported almost exclusively in immunocompromised individuals; indicates a much more serious disease course and requires aggressive systemic antifungal therapy.

primarySymptoms: A painless or minimally painful subcutaneous swelling, typically on the foot or lower leg, that has been present and slowly enlarging over months to years — the most characteristic early feature.
Multiple draining sinuses opening onto the skin overlying the swollen area, discharging serosanguineous or purulent fluid containing visible grains.
Colored grains in the sinus discharge — black, dark brown, or white depending on the causative fungal species — which are visible to the naked eye and are pathognomonic when present.
Progressive deformation of the affected limb as the infection expands through subcutaneous tissue, fascia, and muscle, distorting normal anatomy.
Firm, woody induration of the surrounding tissue from fibrosis and granulomatous reaction, which progressively limits normal movement and joint function.
Intermittent partial closure and reopening of sinuses, with periods of increased discharge following minor trauma or pressure on the swelling.
Bone involvement producing localized bony tenderness, periosteal thickening, or visible deformity of the foot architecture on examination or imaging.
Secondary bacterial infection of sinus tracts causing acute pain, increased warmth, erythema, and purulent discharge during superinfection episodes.
Regional lymphadenopathy in the inguinal or popliteal nodes draining the affected extremity, present in a minority of cases.
Limb disability and inability to bear weight in advanced cases where bone destruction and joint involvement have occurred.
earlyWarningSigns: A small, painless, slowly growing firm nodule on the foot or hand in a person who works barefoot in soil or with plant material in a tropical endemic region.
First appearance of a draining sinus over a foot or limb mass in an agricultural worker, even before grains are visible in the discharge.
Persistent swelling of the foot or leg that does not resolve despite courses of antibiotics — suggesting eumycetoma rather than bacterial infection.
Dark or colored particles noted in the wound discharge from a chronic foot wound in a resident of an endemic area.
emergencySigns: Acute sepsis with high fever, rigors, and systemic illness during a severe secondary bacterial superinfection of sinus tracts — requires urgent hospitalization and intravenous antibiotics.
Rapidly spreading cellulitis proximal to a known mycetoma lesion, with lymphangitis or lymphadenitis suggesting ascending bacterial infection.
Loss of distal sensation or motor function in the foot, suggesting nerve involvement or compartment syndrome from extensive tissue infiltration.
Sudden marked increase in swelling with severe pain in a previously low-grade lesion — may signal bacterial superinfection, hematoma, or deep abscess requiring urgent surgical drainage.

causes.description: The most common cause globally; M. mycetomatis lives as a saprophyte in soil and on thorny plants in endemic regions. Puncture wounds from thorns, splinters, or sharp stones inoculate the fungus into the subcutaneous tissue of barefoot agricultural workers, where it evades immune clearance and establishes a chronic infection.

causes.cause: Implantation of Scedosporium boydii or other white-grain fungi
causes.description: Scedosporium boydii (Pseudallescheria boydii) is the most common eumycetoma agent in temperate regions; like M. mycetomatis, it is introduced by traumatic soil or plant material implantation, most often on the foot, hand, or back.

causes.cause: Barefoot exposure to contaminated soil in endemic regions
causes.description: Walking barefoot on agricultural land in the mycetoma belt provides repeated micro-trauma and fungal inoculation opportunities; the foot accounts for 70% of all mycetoma cases, directly reflecting this exposure pattern.

causes.cause: Agricultural and manual labor involving soil and plant contact
causes.description: Farmers, shepherds, subsistence agricultural workers, and field laborers in endemic regions have sustained, repeated exposure to soil fungal agents and traumatic plant material; occupational exposure explains the male predominance and young adult peak incidence.

causes.cause: Delayed immune containment of implanted fungus
causes.description: Once implanted, filamentous fungi form protective grains — compact hyphal aggregates surrounded by cement matrix — that shield them from host immune effectors including neutrophils and macrophages. This immune evasion strategy allows the infection to persist indefinitely without treatment.

riskFactors.factor: Residence in or travel to the mycetoma belt
riskFactors.description: Endemic in Sudan, Somalia, Mauritania, Senegal, India (Rajasthan, Tamil Nadu), Mexico, and Yemen; risk is negligible outside these regions except in returned travelers or immigrants with relevant occupational histories.

riskFactors.factor: Barefoot agricultural work in endemic regions
riskFactors.description: The combination of barefoot exposure and soil/plant contact in endemic areas constitutes the primary risk environment; protective footwear is the single most important individual preventive measure and essentially eliminates foot-entry risk.

riskFactors.factor: Male sex and young adult age
riskFactors.description: Men aged 20–40 engaged in outdoor agricultural work are the predominant group in all endemic series, with a male-to-female ratio of approximately 3:1; this likely reflects occupational exposure patterns rather than intrinsic biological susceptibility.

Average time from symptom onset to confirmed diagnosis in endemic settings exceeds 5–7 years; this prolonged delay allows the infection to advance from superficial subcutaneous involvement to bone destruction, dramatically worsening prognosis and increasing amputation risk.

HIV infection, malnutrition, and corticosteroid use are associated with more aggressive and rapidly progressive disease; however, the majority of eumycetoma cases occur in immunocompetent individuals, suggesting that the fungal grain structure itself confers immune protection independent of host status.

Absence of adequate footwear in economically disadvantaged rural communities in endemic regions is a direct modifiable risk factor; subsidized footwear programs and education campaigns form part of WHO-recommended prevention strategies.

M. mycetomatis and other eumycetoma agents are enriched in semi-arid clay soils and on Acacia thorn scrub vegetation typical of the Sudano-Sahelian climate zone; the geographic risk closely matches the distribution of these soil and vegetation types.

affectedDemographics: Young adult men aged 20–40 in rural agricultural communities in endemic regions
Barefoot farmers, shepherds, and manual laborers in sub-Saharan Africa, South Asia, and Latin America
Residents of the mycetoma belt: Sudan, Somalia, India (especially Rajasthan), Mexico, Mauritania, Senegal, and Yemen
Travelers and immigrants from endemic regions who develop chronic foot or limb swelling months to years after return
Immunocompromised individuals with any soil exposure in endemic areas
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of eumycetoma rests on identifying the clinical triad of mass, sinuses, and grains, followed by laboratory confirmation of the fungal causative agent. Visual identification of grains in sinus discharge is the first step: their color (black, dark brown, or white-yellow), size, and consistency provide initial species grouping. Microscopy of crushed grains prepared in potassium hydroxide (KOH) reveals fungal hyphae 4–6 micrometers in diameter with a cement matrix — distinguishing eumycetoma from actinomycetoma, in which grains contain thinner bacterial filaments. Fungal culture on Sabouraud dextrose agar is the traditional method for species identification, but growth of M. mycetomatis and related dematiaceous fungi is slow (4–8 weeks) and culture yield from clinical grains is variable (40–70% in expert centers). PCR-based species identification from grain material is significantly more sensitive (>90%) and faster (days rather than weeks) and is increasingly recommended by the Mycetoma Research Centre (MRC) in Sudan as the standard confirmatory test. Imaging is essential for staging the extent of disease: plain radiography detects bone involvement (periosteal reaction, cavities, sclerosis) in intermediate to advanced cases, while MRI is the gold standard for assessing the degree of soft tissue, fascial, and bone infiltration before surgery. CT may be used for complex cases with uncertain anatomical extent. The differential diagnosis principally requires distinguishing eumycetoma from actinomycetoma (which has different treatment implications), as well as from botryomycosis (bacterial grain-forming infection), tuberculosis of bone, fibrosarcoma, and other chronic granulomatous infections. Histopathology of biopsy specimens demonstrating rounded fungal grains with thick cement matrix embedded in granulomatous and fibrous tissue is confirmatory.

diagnosticTests.test: Clinical examination and grain color identification
diagnosticTests.purpose: Primary evaluation — presence of the triad (mass, sinuses, grains) with grain color classification distinguishes eumycetoma (dark or white grains) from actinomycetoma (yellow, red, or white grains) at the bedside.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The clinician examines the lesion and attempts to express grains from a sinus; grains can be viewed with a hand lens or directly on a white gauze pad. Results are immediate and guide laboratory work-up.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies fungal hyphae (4–6 μm diameter, with cement matrix) in eumycetoma versus thin bacterial filaments in actinomycetoma, separating the two forms within minutes.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A grain is crushed on a glass slide, mixed with 10–20% KOH solution, and examined under a microscope by laboratory staff; results are available within an hour.

diagnosticTests.test: Fungal culture on Sabouraud dextrose agar
diagnosticTests.purpose: Species identification for guiding antifungal drug selection; cultures are incubated for 4–8 weeks for slow-growing dematiaceous fungi.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Grains or tissue are placed on culture media in the laboratory; you will not need to return for the culture result yourself — the clinician reviews results and contacts you. Culture takes 4–8 weeks.

diagnosticTests.test: PCR species identification from grain material
diagnosticTests.purpose: Most sensitive method for identifying the causative fungal species (>90% sensitivity); turnaround time days rather than weeks; increasingly available at reference centers.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Grain or tissue material is sent to a reference laboratory; molecular results are available in 3–7 days. Not universally available in resource-limited endemic settings.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold standard imaging for staging soft tissue, fascial, and bone extent of eumycetoma before medical or surgical treatment; MRI shows characteristic 'dot-in-circle' sign in approximately 60% of cases, which is highly specific for mycetoma.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You lie still in an MRI scanner for 30–60 minutes; no radiation exposure. Intravenous gadolinium contrast may be used. Results are reviewed by the specialist team.

diagnosticTests.test: Plain radiography of the affected limb
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects bone involvement — periosteal reaction, cortical erosion, lytic cavities, sclerosis — available in most endemic settings and sufficient for initial bone staging.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard X-rays of the foot or affected region; quick and painless, results available immediately.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms the diagnosis by demonstrating granulomatous inflammation surrounding characteristic fungal grains with cement matrix; distinguishes eumycetoma from other granulomatous lesions.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small tissue biopsy is taken under local anesthesia; tissue is processed and results take 3–7 days. The procedure is performed by a surgeon or dermatologist.

treatmentOverview: Eumycetoma treatment is one of the most challenging in tropical medicine and requires a combination of prolonged antifungal therapy and surgery, with outcomes dependent heavily on the causative species and stage of disease at presentation. Itraconazole is the most extensively used antifungal agent for eumycetoma and remains the WHO-recommended first-line treatment; it is given orally at 400 mg daily (in two divided doses after meals) in cycles of 3–6 months, often continued for 12–24 months or longer depending on response. Response rates with itraconazole alone vary widely by species: white-grain eumycetoma (Scedosporium boydii, Acremonium) tends to respond better, with reported cure rates of 30–55%, while black-grain eumycetoma caused by M. mycetomatis responds poorly to antifungals alone, with medical cure rates of only 25–35% even with prolonged treatment. Voriconazole, a second-generation triazole with broader activity against dematiaceous fungi, has shown higher in vitro activity against M. mycetomatis and is increasingly used as first-line or salvage therapy; clinical cure rates of 45–60% have been reported in M. mycetomatis eumycetoma in combination with surgery in the best series. Surgical debulking — wide excision of the fungal mass while preserving viable tissue — combined with antifungal therapy substantially improves outcomes compared with antifungal therapy alone; for localized early disease, surgery followed by antifungal therapy achieves cure rates of 60–75%. Amputation rates in late-presenting cases with extensive bone destruction, particularly with M. mycetomatis, remain as high as 25–30% in sub-Saharan African series despite antifungal treatment. Novel agents under investigation include the combination of fosmanogepix (antifungal with unique activity against M. mycetomatis) in clinical trials at the MRC Sudan, and there is ongoing interest in the addition of surgery to voriconazole as a standard combination. Treatment monitoring uses clinical assessment (reduction of mass size, closure of sinuses, disappearance of grain discharge) supplemented by serial MRI; microbiological cure requires negative culture and PCR from post-treatment biopsy specimens.

medicalTreatments.name: Itraconazole (oral)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line WHO-recommended antifungal for eumycetoma; given 400 mg daily in two divided doses after meals to maximize absorption. Typically administered in cycles separated by a drug holiday to reduce hepatotoxicity risk; total treatment duration 12–24 months or more. Therapeutic drug monitoring (plasma itraconazole levels) is recommended when available as absorption is highly variable.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Medical cure rates of 30–55% for white-grain eumycetoma; approximately 25–35% for M. mycetomatis black-grain disease; substantially improved when combined with surgical debulking to 60–75% in favorable cases.

medicalTreatments.name: Voriconazole (oral or IV)
medicalTreatments.description: Second-generation triazole with superior in vitro activity against Madurella mycetomatis compared with itraconazole; given 200 mg twice daily orally in adults; used increasingly as first-line for M. mycetomatis eumycetoma and as salvage therapy after itraconazole failure. Requires monitoring for hepatotoxicity and visual disturbances.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical response rates of 45–65% reported in case series for M. mycetomatis eumycetoma; superior to itraconazole against M. mycetomatis in comparative studies at the MRC Sudan.

medicalTreatments.name: Posaconazole (oral)
medicalTreatments.description: Broad-spectrum triazole used as second-line or combination therapy for eumycetoma when itraconazole and voriconazole are insufficient; also active against a range of dematiaceous fungi. Delayed-release tablets (300 mg once daily) improve pharmacokinetics compared with oral suspension.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Limited series show response rates of 40–55% in salvage settings for refractory eumycetoma; most experience comes from compassionate use and case series.

medicalTreatments.name: Surgical debulking (wide local excision)
medicalTreatments.description: Removes the bulk of fungal grain-containing tissue while preserving neurovascular structures and viable healthy tissue; performed under general or regional anesthesia. Reduces the fungal burden that antifungal drugs must eliminate and improves cure rates significantly when combined with adequate antifungal therapy pre- and post-operatively.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Combined surgery plus antifungal therapy achieves cure rates of 60–75% for localized early-to-intermediate disease, compared with 25–35% with antifungals alone.

surgicalOptions.name: Wide local excision (debulking)
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical removal of the mycetoma mass and sinus tracts with adequate surgical margins, preserving viable surrounding tissue; performed in combination with antifungal therapy before and after surgery.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 60–75% cure when combined with adequate antifungal therapy in early-to-intermediate disease without extensive bone involvement

surgicalOptions.name: Amputation
surgicalOptions.description: Reserved for advanced cases with extensive bone destruction, failed medical and conservative surgical management, or uncontrollable secondary bacterial infection; particularly applicable to late-presenting M. mycetomatis eumycetoma with total foot destruction.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Definitive in eliminating infection in the affected limb; amputation rates of 25–30% reported in late-presenting cases in sub-Saharan African centers

alternativeTreatments.name: Terbinafine (combination or monotherapy in selected cases)
alternativeTreatments.description: An allylamine antifungal active against some eumycetoma agents; occasionally used in combination with itraconazole for white-grain eumycetoma in resource-limited settings; not established as standard therapy but reported in case series.

alternativeTreatments.name: Traditional wound management practices
alternativeTreatments.description: In some endemic communities, traditional healers apply herbal poultices to mycetoma lesions; these do not alter the fungal infection but may provide symptomatic relief from secondary bacterial superinfection. Patients using traditional practices should be encouraged to seek concurrent medical antifungal treatment.

whySeeSpecialist: Eumycetoma requires specialist input at the time of diagnosis. An infectious disease specialist or mycologist experienced with mycetoma should confirm the causative species (which determines the antifungal regimen), interpret MRI staging, and plan the combined medical-surgical approach. Given the poor outcome of antifungal monotherapy for M. mycetomatis, early surgical consultation is essential in all but the most superficial presentations. Delay in specialist referral is one of the primary drivers of the high amputation rates in endemic settings. Patients in non-endemic countries should be referred to centers with tropical or mycology expertise, as general practitioners may not have experience with this rare condition.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek an infectious disease physician or clinical mycologist with specific experience in subcutaneous fungal infections, ideally at a center with access to PCR-based species identification, therapeutic drug monitoring of itraconazole or voriconazole, and MRI imaging capability. In endemic regions, the Mycetoma Research Centre (MRC) in Khartoum, Sudan, is the world's leading referral center and has established a global mycetoma registry. Outside endemic regions, referral to a tropical medicine unit at a teaching hospital is appropriate. An orthopedic or plastic surgeon experienced in mycetoma excision should be part of the treatment team.
preventionStrategies: Wear protective, thick-soled footwear when working in soil or with plant material in endemic regions — this is the most impactful preventive measure at the individual level.
Avoid walking barefoot in agricultural fields, near Acacia thorn scrub, or on dusty soil in endemic areas.
Cover wounds, lacerations, and skin abrasions immediately to prevent soil contamination in endemic settings.
Seek early medical assessment for any slowly growing, painless swelling on the foot or leg in an endemic region — diagnosis before bone involvement improves cure rates substantially.
Support community-level educational programs about early presentation and mycetoma recognition in endemic areas, as awareness reduces the 5–7-year diagnostic delay.
Advocate for improved access to diagnosis and antifungal treatment in rural endemic communities, consistent with WHO Neglected Tropical Disease roadmap goals.
lifestyleModifications: Consistently wear protective footwear in all outdoor activities in endemic regions; make footwear access a family priority, particularly for children who are beginning to work in fields.
Promptly clean and dress all foot wounds or thorn punctures in endemic areas to reduce the risk of fungal implantation.

Adhere strictly to the prescribed antifungal regimen — itraconazole requires consistent twice-daily dosing with fatty food for adequate absorption; intermittent or incomplete dosing dramatically reduces effectiveness.
Attend all follow-up appointments for clinical and imaging-based monitoring of treatment response; eumycetoma treatment spans 12–24 months and requires sustained engagement.
Avoid alcohol consumption during antifungal therapy, as both itraconazole and voriconazole are hepatically metabolized and concurrent alcohol use increases hepatotoxicity risk.
Protect the affected limb from trauma during treatment; recurrent micro-trauma may reactivate dormant foci and introduce secondary bacterial infection.

recommended: Take itraconazole with a fatty meal or full-fat dairy to maximize drug absorption — absorption increases 2–3-fold under fed compared with fasted conditions
A protein-adequate diet to support wound healing, immune function, and tissue recovery during the prolonged treatment period
Adequate caloric intake, particularly in malnourished patients in endemic settings where undernutrition is common and affects both immune function and drug pharmacokinetics
Vitamin C from fruits and vegetables to support collagen synthesis and skin repair after surgical debulking
avoid: Alcohol during antifungal therapy due to hepatotoxicity risk with itraconazole and voriconazole
Fasting or very low-fat meals immediately before or after itraconazole dosing, which reduces absorption and therapeutic plasma levels

exerciseGuidelines: Activity should be modulated according to disease stage. In early and intermediate disease undergoing antifungal treatment, normal daily activities are permitted but the affected limb should be protected from mechanical trauma and excessive weight-bearing stress when bone involvement is present. After surgical debulking, physiotherapy-guided rehabilitation is recommended to maintain joint range of motion and prevent contractures. In advanced disease with significant bone and joint destruction, weight-bearing limitation may be necessary and should be guided by the orthopedic surgical team. Full return to agricultural work requires cure confirmation by MRI and clinical examination.
prognosis: The prognosis of eumycetoma depends critically on the causative species and the stage at presentation. Early, superficial disease diagnosed before bone involvement and treated with combined antifungal therapy plus surgery achieves cure rates of 60–75% in optimal center data. Late-presenting M. mycetomatis eumycetoma with extensive bone destruction has a poor prognosis: antifungal cure rates rarely exceed 25–35%, and amputation rates remain 25–30% in African series despite prolonged treatment. White-grain eumycetoma (Scedosporium boydii, Acremonium) generally carries a better prognosis than black-grain M. mycetomatis disease, with higher antifungal response rates. Recurrence after clinical cure is documented in approximately 15–20% of surgically treated cases, requiring repeat antifungal courses. The overall mortality from eumycetoma is low — death is rare from the fungal infection itself — but the disease causes profound disability, amputation, and loss of livelihood in young working adults, with major economic and social consequences for affected families in endemic communities.
recoveryTimeline: Response to antifungal therapy is slow and should be assessed clinically every 3 months (reduction in mass size, sinus closure, cessation of grain discharge) and radiologically every 6 months by MRI. Meaningful clinical response typically requires 6–12 months of continuous antifungal therapy. Surgical debulking, when performed, is usually planned after 3–6 months of antifungal pretreatment to reduce grain burden and facilitate complete excision. Post-surgical antifungal therapy continues for 12–24 months. Parasitological cure (negative post-treatment biopsy culture and PCR) is the definitive endpoint; it may take 18–36 months from the start of combined treatment in favorable cases. Patients who have not achieved significant clinical response after 12 months of appropriate antifungal therapy should have species identification reviewed and consider switching or escalating to voriconazole.
complications: Bone destruction and osteomyelitis — M. mycetomatis and other causative fungi invade bone, causing cortical erosion, lytic cavities, periosteal sclerosis, and ultimately structural destruction of foot architecture; occurs in approximately 60–70% of cases at presentation in endemic series.

Amputation — advanced eumycetoma with total bone and joint destruction unresponsive to medical therapy leads to below-knee or foot amputation in 25–30% of late-presenting cases; amputation rate is highest with M. mycetomatis black-grain disease.

Secondary bacterial superinfection — sinus tracts provide a portal for bacteria, causing recurrent acute cellulitis, abscess formation, and sometimes ascending limb sepsis requiring hospitalization and intravenous antibiotics.

Functional disability and limb deformity — progressive destruction of tendons, joints, and bones produces severe deformity and loss of weight-bearing function that impairs mobility and prevents normal occupational activity even before amputation is required.

Drug-related hepatotoxicity — prolonged itraconazole or voriconazole therapy causes significant liver enzyme elevation in 5–15% of patients; regular liver function monitoring every 3 months is required and dose reduction or drug substitution may be necessary.
Recurrence after treatment — approximately 15–20% of surgically treated patients experience recurrence within 5 years; these cases require repeat antifungal courses and surgical re-evaluation.

supportResources.name: WHO — Mycetoma: Fact Sheet
supportResources.description: WHO patient-facing overview of mycetoma as a neglected tropical disease, including eumycetoma and actinomycetoma, with information on global burden and control strategies.

supportResources.name: Mycetoma Research Centre, University of Khartoum, Sudan
supportResources.description: The world's leading center for mycetoma research, diagnosis, and treatment; provides patient resources, specialist consultation information, and links to the global mycetoma registry.

supportResources.name: DNDi — Mycetoma Programme
supportResources.description: Drugs for Neglected Diseases initiative (DNDi) program developing new treatments for mycetoma including eumycetoma, with updates on clinical trial progress including fosmanogepix.

sources.title: van de Sande WW. Global burden of human mycetoma: a systematic review and meta-analysis. PLOS Neglected Tropical Diseases 2013; 7(11):e2550
Ahmed AA et al. Mycetoma: An Updated Review of Etiology, Diagnosis, and Treatment. PLOS Neglected Tropical Diseases 2017; 11(9):e0005637
Nenoff P et al. Eumycetoma caused by Madurella mycetomatis: a difficult-to-treat chronic fungal infection. Mycoses 2015; 58(4):197-210
Fahal AH et al. The Mycetoma Research Centre, University of Khartoum Hospital: a 15-year institutional series of 1,010 mycetoma patients. PLOS Neglected Tropical Diseases 2018; 12(6):e0006424
El-Sobky M et al. MRI of the foot and ankle in mycetoma: a prospective study. British Journal of Radiology 2007; 80(960):986-991
WHO — Ending the neglect to attain the Sustainable Development Goals: a road map for neglected tropical diseases 2021–2030

confusedWithConditions.name: Actinomycetoma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Actinomycetoma is caused by filamentous bacteria (Nocardia, Streptomyces, Actinomadura), produces thinner bacterial filaments in grains on KOH microscopy, and — critically — responds to prolonged antibiotic therapy (co-trimoxazole, amikacin combinations), making correct species differentiation essential before treatment.

confusedWithConditions.name: Sporotrichosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Sporotrichosis causes nodular lymphangitic lesions ascending the limb along lymphatics after thorn implantation, without grain formation or draining sinuses; it responds well to itraconazole 200 mg daily for 3–6 months — a much shorter course than eumycetoma.

confusedWithConditions.name: Cutaneous leishmaniasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Cutaneous leishmaniasis produces ulcerating nodules with a characteristic crater appearance and is caused by Leishmania protozoa transmitted by sandfly bites, not fungal implantation; no grains or sinuses are present and diagnosis is by smear showing amastigotes or PCR.

confusedWithConditions.name: Blastomycosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Blastomycosis causes systemic infection with primary pulmonary disease and secondary skin lesions that ulcerate or form verrucous plaques, but does not produce grains or draining sinuses; diagnosis is by culture or antigen detection rather than grain microscopy.

relatedConditions.name: Actinomycetoma
relatedConditions.relevance: The bacterial form of mycetoma — clinically identical at presentation, distinguished by KOH microscopy and culture; treated with long-term antibiotic combinations rather than antifungals, making the distinction between the two forms the most critical decision in mycetoma management.

relatedConditions.name: Sporotrichosis
relatedConditions.relevance: A subcutaneous fungal infection also acquired through traumatic implantation of plant material; causes lymphangitic nodular lesions rather than grain-forming sinuses, and responds well to itraconazole — a related but distinct clinical entity.

relatedConditions.name: Leishmaniasis
relatedConditions.relevance: An important differential and co-endemic condition in the mycetoma belt; cutaneous leishmaniasis causes chronic skin ulcers in the same geographic regions and patient population as eumycetoma.

relatedConditions.name: Chromoblastomycosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Another chronic subcutaneous fungal infection acquired through traumatic plant implantation in tropical settings, producing verrucous skin plaques; distinct from eumycetoma in its pathology and treatment approach.

faqs.question: What is eumycetoma?
faqs.answer: Eumycetoma is a chronic fungal infection of the skin and underlying tissue caused by filamentous fungi implanted through a thorn or splinter wound, typically on the foot. It produces a slowly growing swelling with discharging sinuses containing visible grain-like fungal particles.

faqs.question: What causes eumycetoma?
faqs.answer: Eumycetoma is caused by filamentous fungi, most commonly Madurella mycetomatis, that live in soil. The fungus is introduced into skin and tissue through a puncture wound — usually from a thorn or sharp object in barefoot agricultural workers in tropical endemic regions.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of eumycetoma?
faqs.answer: The main symptoms are a painless swelling on the foot or leg, multiple draining sinuses (tunnels through skin), and visible grain-like particles in the discharge. The swelling grows slowly over months to years and can deform the affected limb if untreated.

faqs.question: How is eumycetoma diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis involves identifying grains in sinus discharge under microscopy, confirming fungal hyphae (rather than bacteria), and species identification by culture or PCR. MRI is used to assess how deep the infection has spread into tissue and bone before treatment planning.

faqs.question: How is eumycetoma treated?
faqs.answer: Treatment uses prolonged antifungal therapy — itraconazole 400 mg daily for 12–24 months, or voriconazole for Madurella infections — combined with surgical removal of the fungal mass. Antifungals alone cure only 25–35% of cases; surgery significantly improves outcomes.

faqs.question: Is eumycetoma curable?
faqs.answer: Eumycetoma can be cured in early-to-intermediate stages with combined antifungal therapy and surgery, achieving 60–75% cure rates. Advanced disease with extensive bone destruction is much harder to cure, with amputation required in about 25–30% of late cases.

faqs.question: What is the difference between eumycetoma and actinomycetoma?
faqs.answer: Eumycetoma is caused by fungi; actinomycetoma is caused by filamentous bacteria. They look identical clinically but are treated differently — eumycetoma needs antifungals, actinomycetoma needs antibiotics. Microscopy of grains distinguishes them and is essential before starting treatment.

faqs.question: Where is eumycetoma most common?
faqs.answer: Eumycetoma is most common in the mycetoma belt — a tropical and subtropical region including Sudan, Somalia, India (Rajasthan), Mexico, Mauritania, Senegal, and Yemen. Sudan has the highest documented burden globally. It is rare in temperate countries.

faqs.question: How do you get eumycetoma?
faqs.answer: You get eumycetoma when a fungus that lives in soil enters a puncture wound — typically from a thorn, splinter, or sharp stone — in the foot or hand. It cannot spread from person to person and is not inherited. Walking barefoot in endemic rural areas is the main risk.

faqs.question: How long does eumycetoma treatment take?
faqs.answer: Treatment is prolonged. Antifungal therapy typically continues for 12–24 months, sometimes longer. Surgery is planned after 3–6 months of pretreatment antifungals. Patients require follow-up every 3–6 months with clinical and MRI assessment throughout treatment.

faqs.question: What are the grains in eumycetoma?
faqs.answer: The grains are compact clumps of fungal hyphae surrounded by a protective cement matrix. They measure 0.2–5 mm in diameter and discharge through sinus tracts in the skin. Their color — black for Madurella mycetomatis, white or yellow for Scedosporium and others — helps identify the fungal species.

faqs.question: Can eumycetoma lead to amputation?
faqs.answer: Yes. In advanced cases where the fungus has destroyed bone and is not responding to antifungal therapy, amputation of the foot or lower limb may be the only option. This occurs in about 25–30% of late-presenting cases in sub-Saharan Africa.

faqs.question: Does eumycetoma spread to other parts of the body?
faqs.answer: Eumycetoma spreads locally — progressively deeper into tissue, fascia, muscle, and bone at the original site. Systemic blood-borne spread to other organs is extremely rare and occurs mainly in severely immunocompromised patients.

faqs.question: What antifungal is used for eumycetoma?
faqs.answer: Itraconazole 400 mg daily is the WHO-recommended first-line antifungal for eumycetoma. Voriconazole is increasingly used, especially for Madurella mycetomatis, which responds better to it. Treatment continues for 12–24 months and is monitored by drug level testing where available.

faqs.question: Is eumycetoma contagious?
faqs.answer: No. Eumycetoma does not spread from person to person. The fungal organism lives in soil, not in humans, and only causes infection when physically implanted by injury. There is no risk to household contacts or healthcare workers from a patient with eumycetoma.

faqs.question: Can eumycetoma be prevented?
faqs.answer: Wearing protective footwear in endemic regions is the most effective prevention. Avoiding barefoot exposure on agricultural land, covering wounds promptly, and seeking early medical care for any chronic foot swelling are the key preventive steps.

faqs.question: Why does eumycetoma progress so slowly?
faqs.answer: The causative fungi form grain structures — compact hyphal aggregates surrounded by cement matrix — that shield them from the immune system. Host defenses (neutrophils, macrophages) cannot effectively penetrate the grain to kill the fungus, allowing the infection to persist and expand very slowly over years.

faqs.question: What does an eumycetoma look like?
faqs.answer: Eumycetoma appears as a painless, woody swelling — usually on the foot — with multiple small opening holes (sinuses) on the overlying skin surface that discharge fluid containing tiny visible grain particles. The skin is distorted and the affected area enlarges and deforms over years.

faqs.question: Is surgery always needed for eumycetoma?
faqs.answer: Surgery combined with antifungals gives significantly better results than antifungals alone. For small early lesions, antifungals may be tried first. For most established cases, surgical debulking followed by prolonged antifungal therapy is the recommended approach to maximize cure rates.

faqs.question: What happens if eumycetoma is left untreated?
faqs.answer: Without treatment, eumycetoma progresses relentlessly over years. The swelling enlarges, more sinuses form, and the infection invades deeper tissues including bone, causing progressive destruction of foot architecture, severe deformity, functional disability, and eventually may necessitate amputation.

metaDescription: Eumycetoma is a chronic fungal infection causing foot swelling, sinuses and grain discharge. Learn causes, antifungal treatment and surgical management.
